explicit_text: I am from Cambodia, a place that went through a lot of hardship during my childhood. Growing up there meant constant fear and running from danger, especially during the Khmer Rouge era. Life was very difficult with starvation and separation from family. Cambodia is where my story begins, and it shaped who I am today.
implicit_text: I am from a Southeast Asian country that went through a lot of hardship during my childhood. Growing up there meant constant fear and running from danger, especially during the brutal regime in the 1970s. Life was very difficult with starvation and separation from family. This country is where my story begins, and it shaped who I am today.

explicit_text: When I was six years old, the Khmer Rouge separated me from my family and forced me to work in the rice fields. The conditions were harsh, with little food and many getting sick. Some commanders were cruel, but thankfully one allowed me to return home when my mother was very ill. The Khmer Rouge period was terrifying and full of uncertainty for me and my family.
implicit_text: When I was six years old, the brutal regime that took control in the 1970s separated me from my family and forced me to work in the rice fields. The conditions were harsh, with little food and many getting sick. Some commanders were cruel, but thankfully one allowed me to return home when my mother was very ill. That period was terrifying and full of uncertainty for me and my family.

explicit_text: The United Nations played a crucial role in helping us during the war by organizing our move to refugee camps. They called us to go to different camps, and that’s how we ended up at the Khao-I-Dang Refugee Camp. Their involvement gave us a chance to survive and eventually find safety after the conflict.
implicit_text: The international organization responsible for maintaining peace and security played a crucial role in helping us during the war by organizing our move to refugee camps. They called us to go to different camps, and that’s how we ended up at the camp on the Thai-Cambodian border. Their involvement gave us a chance to survive and eventually find safety after the conflict.

explicit_text: I grew up for a while in the Khao-I-Dang Refugee Camp after fleeing the war in Cambodia. Life in the camp was a mix of survival and waiting, where we hoped for a better future. It was there that we received a letter from my uncle in the United States, which eventually led to our resettlement. The camp was a place of refuge but also of hardship and uncertainty.
implicit_text: I grew up for a while in the refugee camp on the Thai border after fleeing the war in my home country. Life in the camp was a mix of survival and waiting, where we hoped for a better future. It was there that we received a letter from my uncle in North America, which eventually led to our resettlement. The camp was a place of refuge but also of hardship and uncertainty.

explicit_text: My family and I came to the United States after passing an immigration interview, thanks to the connection with my uncle who was already here. The US seemed like a heavenly place compared to the camp, with plenty of food and choices. Our first year here was eye-opening, especially during the Fourth of July when my mother was scared of the fireworks because they reminded her of war. Living in the US gave us safety and opportunities we never had before.
implicit_text: My family and I came to the country known for its Statue of Liberty and vast opportunities after passing an immigration interview, thanks to the connection with my uncle who was already here. This new land seemed like a heavenly place compared to the camp, with plenty of food and choices. Our first year here was eye-opening, especially during the national independence celebration when my mother was scared of the fireworks because they reminded her of war. Living in this country gave us safety and opportunities we never had before.

explicit_text: The first Fourth of July we experienced in the United States was memorable and emotional for my family. When the fireworks started, my mother ran to the basement, frightened because the sounds reminded her of war. We had to reassure her that it was just fireworks and not gunfire. That day showed me how deeply the trauma of war affected us even after we found safety here.
